speaker: Robert Hieronimus
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: That is the best stuff. I went to my father-in-law who was a major philanthropist in Baltimore. And I said, look, Dad, here's a situation. Trying to help these guys, and I think it's important to get the black leadership in this town that has money to get behind them and support them. And he said, you're kidding. He says, you're not going to be able to do that. And I said, that's absurd. I mean, these guys are millionaires. Give me, unless the five or six millionaires in this town are guys that are very wealthy that have a social responsibility theme. And I'm sure if I talk to them, you know, down to that and he said, OK, I'll make a bet with you. He says, whatever money they give, I'll double it. How much money do you think we raised if you guys, nothing? Nothing. They're not going to mention their names. It's too embarrassing. These are black leaders in the city. We couldn't raise a dollar. And this was to take these guys to the White House. They would have been given the publicity of making it possible, you know, after we had done all the work and said it all along. All we needed was $10,000. And all those six, we couldn't get any. I had to chase them for months. It was truly embarrassing. It was so embarrassing that I went back to him and he said, OK, how many did you get? And I said, none. He says, you didn't get a nickel, did you? I said, no, I didn't. And he chipped in the money anyway and made it possible. But he said, well, he learned a major lesson here. I said, if you're going to help these folks, these other humans, they got to start helping themselves. The black community has to help them. He says, if not, you're always going to be whitey. You're always going to be on the outside. And you're always going to be helping them. And it's not going to change anything. He says, so this is an important lesson for you to learn. It was crushing because seldom do it. It does one thing that you know far more than your father-in-law who knows a great deal about this area. I don't need to find out that he had it pegged. Then he related the experiences he had personally over the last 30 years. He said, it's always the same thing. I'll go in there and I'll lay down whatever million that's needed to do something. And who do you think the credit goes to? It goes to a black organization or it goes to part of that community or whatever and like that. And they get the credit. The thing gets done two years later. They're back to you. And he said, after 30 years of it, we don't do it anymore in that way. So he said that in order and he was right and he was right. So it's those kind of things that make you feel like not only are you naive and I'm talking about myself. We can have all these great feelings about trying to change things but it takes careful planning and a monstrous will. I mean you never take no. Someone says no to you. You don't get upset. You just you march on and you just do it again. You have to be relentless and that's how we succeed it. We didn't have it wasn't until 90 spring of 95 when Leon had made in and in that all the sudden the sunpapers and everybody else was friggin' paying attention. Where were they when we needed them so desperately back in 91, 92, 93, 94. It was only until it was a fate of complete that they come in step in and say, oh yeah we're lending the help and hand here. And most amazing it wasn't until 96, 97 that we got the sports people of the sunpaper to give any help at all and it happened to come through two very knowledgeable, highly healed women who said we can't let this happen to Geraldine Day. We can't let her sit in a cold apartment with no food. Can you name these women? Sure. Let's put that on pause. Okay. The people that helped enormously at the sunpapers were the sports editor Molly Dunham, DUNHAM and Sally McKee at the sunpapers and what they did came in about 1997 when Geraldine Day was in deplorable situation with their help all together and I think we can say it now because most of it has been, most of it she's had to spend to keep her act together over $25,000 we raised on February 15th, 1997 for that particular event. But they publicized it and they pushed it. They actually even put in the newspapers that people wanted to donate something to donate it directly to them. The sunpapers to write it out to them and they'd see that Geraldine got the checks and I think over $4,000 was raised that added to the other 20-some thousand made a big difference. Really made a big difference. That is the kindest thing that the sunpapers has done and again it didn't come from the eyes that I thought would have had the sensitivity to it had to come through women. Strange, huh? Yeah, different. But if they hadn't, if they hadn't, Geraldine would be in a much worse situation today because we're in a situation again in which she's down to her last, this may sound like a lot of money to you but it's not down to her last $6,000. All that money we raised for. And she's not going out there by and stuff. If her medical bills and her, I mean, $20,000 or $1,000 over this will be, she spread it over two years. It's damn good for people's expenses, especially hers. So we're going to have to do something again. And I don't think we can do it the same way we did before because while we were doing this fundraising, we were opposed in doing this fundraising by Joe Black. Joe Black was a picture for the Brooklyn Dodgers, damn good one. And he basically told us we were embarrassing, majorly baseball by coming out and saying, you know, going over these things, so she's impoverished as medical, so fortunately, he and I have mended our fences just this year. I had to promise in order to do this, the men defense with Joe Black, that I would not, if I'm doing a fundraiser, never do it for naming a specific player or a person like Geraldine. He says because they have an organization called Bat Baseball Assistance Team, which is helping her. And they're saying her a check for like $500 a month and that makes a very big difference in her life. And if that would stop, she'd be in real big trouble. You're still currently involved with an organization for the New Year League. Yes, called the Negro League Baseball Players Association. We meet in Baltimore every, usually every month. What is your position? I'm just called an honorary board member. Can you name some of the other people? Oh, well, the players that are involved are the hundred and some players that are Negro League players, that is played from 1920 to 1950. And of course, that is ones that always attend the meetings are the President and Vice President, Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glen, Bill Cash. There are some others that aren't full-fledged Negro League players that attend those meetings. At the last meeting, let's see, I'm trying to think of, there's a person that helps in a sense of sponsoring these meetings so that we can have them as another white individual's attorneys. His name is Charles Winner. His organization is Pro Bono, then helping for the last since 1995. Somewhere around there and holding this organization together. We meet in his offices once a month and a couple of months ago, we had any Murray, any Murray is behind us with his brother, Charlie. And we are slowly but surely getting some of the other Oriole players involved in this. But then at the same time, there's another thing that may or may not happen in that who is an individual came to us who is going to be inheriting a good deal of money. And their initial idea was to do a film to set the record straight about the Negro League players. And also to have real pensions for these players. As soon as she made these announcements, people within her family who did not have her feelings along these lines hit the roof. We don't know how long it's going to take for them to work out their economic situation. This person is inheriting tens of millions of which she would like to funnel in this direction. You see, every time we make a step in a certain direction, it looks good and it holds up for a while and then people from the outside who have never heard of this come in and say, what do you want to do that? Why do you want to help them? I mean, you know, what they would do for you. That's that kind of thing. And then after a while, these people begin to feel very uneasy because other members and their family and other people are saying, you know, come on, you're not black, why help them and help you. So come on, let's get on with their life. Do other things. So whenever that is resolved, if it is resolved in a positive way, we will be able to finally get a decent pension for the whole of the guys, 170 of them. Not just the rinky dink, few thousand dollars they're going to get per year for major big baseball. But a minimum of 10,000 a year, which isn't a great deal of money, but it's a whole lot more than what they got. So that's something we have in the background that we're working on. And it comes in spurts. I remember this person just the other day, it's just today. And they're still going through hell. There's politics and all of this. When you get into big money, there's all these politics that are involved with people coming through. If here's a better deal for you, why don't you invest in this? Why don't you invest? That's understandable. But hopefully we'll be able to iron this one out one last time. But it's going to take a good year to it. What would you say are the chief contributing factors to the rediscovery of the Negro leagues? Not necessarily your own personal discovery, but the undoubtedly. It was an organization called Saber, the Society for the American Baseball Research. They are really key to this whole thing. It was their research that I read back then, 1990-91, that I began to realize that my father was telling me that these were indeed some of the greatest baseball players ever. So I think, what else did you ask? I was asking about not necessarily your personal experience, but you say, well, we discovery as a whole, people who are really conscious of this. Again, there's a committee, the Negro League Committee of the Saber Society for American Baseball Research is the most qualified group of individuals from the standpoint that they followed this thing for 40, they're my teachers. They're the ones that, and these are mostly all whites, again. And they're the ones that, when I said, hey, I just went and talked to Tom Morris. Tom Morris said to me, these guys weren't so good. They played in band boxes in which 300 feet for the center field. And then these other historians who were true historians, they said, well, where do you get that information? I mean, of course, he got it from some other friend who has his prejudice against blacks. And that's a definite thing. It happens. Pregnance against blacks in this. So from that perspective, I would have to say that that's the organization that did most, has done the most in the spearheaded, this big change, and they get very little credit. I had very little credit. Describe the initial reaction that Leende had prior to getting into the epine. Well, as you'll read in this newsletter thing, I explained, I discussed the whole situation in the deal that he and I had made. And the deal that had I made was a Bob, you know, after I told him that Buck O'Neill this was in the summer of 94. And I told him. I said, I couldn't tell him the name in person who gave me the information. But you're in. You're going to be voted in this year. He didn't believe me. This is 1994. He had been through enough. He was going through enough pain with prostate problems and everything else. So he didn't believe me. The funny thing he did, and this is very much not like Leende, and he said, okay, Bob, I want you to be the person when it happens. When you find out you come to me first, I want to talk to you first. You tell me that I'm in and then I'll believe it. So what happened is at the hospital, they were wheeling them out of some exploratory thing that they were doing with them. He was really a nun to good shape. He had already had this incredible dream that he had already was in the Hall of Fame. At seven o'clock in the morning, he called Geraldine and said, I'm made it, honey, I'm made it. And she reminded them that seven o'clock they haven't even met yet, and of course, he was crestfallen. But he was told, his soul told him, you know, and he believed it. So when he was being wheeled out, we had previously made a deal on the inside with myself and the other players, because I've always felt uneasy in this situation. I'm not a player, right? I'm their friend, but I'm not a player. So we figured out, okay, who do we want to give this honor to? We gave it to Max Mann, who was his teammate, his pitching teammate, which made sense. And it looked good. And it looked good for the media to have some black person telling, but I couldn't even tell these guys what I promised to be on. Because you want these fellows to get the most out of the situation you can. It doesn't do much good if I say, look, I hate to tell you this, but Leon told me I had to tell him from the cameras. But that doesn't help them. It doesn't help the whole image of the thing. So when they wheeled him out, I was standing by, he said, and is the only time he's ever called me Dr. Heranomus. He yelled, Dr. Heranomus, where's Dr. Heranomus? I want to speak to him. So I went over to him and he looked at me and I knew what he was thinking and I said, you're in. And he said, and we had this on videotape. This was really neat. We didn't know we had it on videotape. And he said, no kidding. I said, yep, you're in. So the last thing I saw of him is they were real. He was trying to get up and he was looking at me like, okay, all right, I believe you. I believe you. But he didn't. But he got into the room. He was such a kind guy. And Max Manning bent over and held his hand and said, you made it. You made it. That's when he believed it. But he still said, no kid. He still couldn't handle it. It was truly, and I really was. It was the most joyous moment in this fellow's life. And for the last for six days was the pathetic situation of it. Because I knew it was nip and tuck. I couldn't tell the other guys. I thought he was going to die before March 6 came. I did. I was very worried. And after that, after putting all these other things together for him to celebrate it, I always thought in my heart, he ain't never going to make it. He ain't never going to see these things. It was quite wonderful that he had those six days because he wasn't really going to make it. So that's really what happened. They explained in this newsletter. And I thought I had to set it straight. I've listed all the names of the people that helped. This was up to 1996 somewhere around there. So you won't find their two names. Because they didn't enter the picture until later. They entered it after they realized that year two years, almost after his death, but they realized that this can't go on. So I think about these two women often. Never met him. But they were really on some heroes. You cover it all? I'm going to ask you more questions. Well, I asked me one or two more. We were colder or three. What sticks out in your mind most about the accounts of the National Negro League players had. Like you said, Adair, when you were in the bus ride with them, I guess I'm asking you basically for another anecdote. Something. Something. OK, well, really impressed me was their memories. All these bus trips that they take. And we took a number of bus trips with them. Their memories would be precise. They would remember. They would remember it looks on each other's faces when they were throwing the ball. Which one was a strike? And they would argue over, no, the first two were strikes, not two balls. I mean, things like that. And then you drop the ball. You drop the ball and you lost that game for us. I mean, it was more or less. They would go through the games. They would relive the games play by play. Now I don't know if people do that anymore. But that's truly remarkable. After 30, 40 years to say he came out on the field and have a shoe on right, he bent down to pick it up. And after that, we knew we had him because we could tell by the way he was bending down. He wasn't feeling so hot. I mean, because he would observe everything about, you talk about guys that were fans that watch everything. These guys, they, it was a very strange situation because they not only watched their competition, but they watched the other guys on the team because they were in competition with them. To get out there on the field. Because if they weren't out on the field and somebody else was, they weren't getting paid. So you know, they had no choice but to compete all the time with not just the opposition, but themselves. So it's their memories of all of these exacting things that I was overwhelmed with. The fact that they remembered when they spit, they remembered, they remembered all the little things that most people would think were inconsequential. Because they loved what they were doing. They really loved it. I mean, this is the thing that is so important that most people are not aware of. When you love what you do, you usually do your very best. This may be one of them. Those kind of things have always impressed me because they never, when they told these stories about the games, you could see the games going on in your head. They told them such a motion. That's why we wanted to get them on film. That's why we wanted to get them documented because it's the enthusiasm with which they told them. And they're very upset with the way Americans think Negro League Baseball was. They don't like it at all. They feel that they've been jipped. They weren't clowns. They weren't happy, they weren't lucky. The struggles that they had to come up against. I mean, some of the teams that they play about, they talk about dead balls. You know what dead balls are? Dead balls are when you freeze a baseball. And when they would buy their baseballs, they had to buy their baseballs sometimes from the team in which they were playing against. So what these guys would do is they would take a gross of baseballs and put them in freezers and give them to them just before the game. At that ball, you can't hit. You hit it and it dribbles. It's the most you're going to get out of 100 feet or something like that. So they had their own ways of determining whether they had a dead ball or not because of the kind of people. But you know, I don't know of any major league player that's ever given this any thought. And so the quality of the shenanigans that were done to them, some of them especially dealing with, you know, was legal to throw spit balls. It was legal to throw cut balls, sandpaper balls, slime balls, needle balls, you know, they stick a needle in there. You know, this was football but baseball because when they played it, you know, you didn't block any base path with these guys because if you were blocking the base, then the natural thing to do is to jump up in the air and spike it, you know, to come down on you like that. And then you didn't block bases anymore. You get block bases in white baseball, but not in black baseball. The games that they play on the first basement, which is the same thing. They had to be very careful about standing on the bag because the game that the runner always had was the guy is standing. If the guy is standing in the wrong place on the, on the bag, you got him. You land on their foot, you know, you've been written here. They're famous in their favorite place. And that would put that guy out of commission and you'd get on that first base and go on the second. I mean, that's a desperate, pretty desperate, that's how they played. I mean, I never would want to play against them. Only because of that, because if they had it out for you, you were a dead man. You know, they had some, they had, they had some violence and prejudices against each other. You know, when they called you a nigger, it meant something. I'm not using the term in a derogatory sense. I'm using the term that they use. And they refer to players, certain players. And I've heard Leon say this and I've heard some of the others. They call him he's a nigger. Which means, you know, not only as an asshole, but you could never trust him and he's not even human. He hasn't had, you know, the kind of, of the, well, it doesn't have, it's not the kind of person that you would respect or want to be around with or anything like that. They have a definite, you know, and it's really very strange when I was with a group of them and they, when they come off with this. Because I was always thought, never to use that term. I thought it was a terrible derogatory term and I sure as hell never thought the blacks would use that term. But the disc group did, I don't know if they do anymore, disc group did. And they did it for a very intentional purpose. I mean, I'm hearing Monty Irvin and, and, and others, you know, Hall of Famers saying oh, he's just a nigger. Meaning that the, that, that, I'll give you an example. There are a lot of black entrepreneurs that are not big ones, black entrepreneurs, but small ones. They have tried to make a buck off these players. And we've had some here in Baltimore that have caused terrible situations. And that's who they call niggers. I remember the very first time I heard them say that. I, I, I jumped back and they looked at me and they said, look, this is a person who we don't want to have anything to do with. And that's the term we use and we're always use. So that kind of thing. I think we better stop. Okay. Thank you very much. You're welcome. Sorry.

segments.text: That is the best stuff.
I went to my father-in-law who was a major philanthropist in Baltimore.
And I said, look, Dad, here's a situation.
Trying to help these guys, and I think it's important to get the black leadership in
this town that has money to get behind them and support them.
And he said, you're kidding.
He says, you're not going to be able to do that.
And I said, that's absurd.
I mean, these guys are millionaires.
Give me, unless the five or six millionaires in this town are guys that are very wealthy
that have a social responsibility theme.
And I'm sure if I talk to them, you know, down to that and he said, OK, I'll make a bet
with you.
He says, whatever money they give, I'll double it.
How much money do you think we raised if you guys, nothing?
Nothing.
They're not going to mention their names.
It's too embarrassing.
These are black leaders in the city.
We couldn't raise a dollar.
And this was to take these guys to the White House.
They would have been given the publicity of making it possible, you know, after we had
done all the work and said it all along.
All we needed was $10,000.
And all those six, we couldn't get any.
I had to chase them for months.
It was truly embarrassing.
It was so embarrassing that I went back to him and he said, OK, how many did you get?
And I said, none.
He says, you didn't get a nickel, did you?
I said, no, I didn't.
And he chipped in the money anyway and made it possible.
But he said, well, he learned a major lesson here.
I said, if you're going to help these folks, these other humans, they got to start helping
themselves.
The black community has to help them.
He says, if not, you're always going to be whitey.
You're always going to be on the outside.
And you're always going to be helping them.
And it's not going to change anything.
He says, so this is an important lesson for you to learn.
It was crushing because seldom do it.
It does one thing that you know far more than your father-in-law who knows a great deal
about this area.
I don't need to find out that he had it pegged.
Then he related the experiences he had personally over the last 30 years.
He said, it's always the same thing.
I'll go in there and I'll lay down whatever million that's needed to do something.
And who do you think the credit goes to?
It goes to a black organization or it goes to part of that community or whatever and like
that.
And they get the credit.
The thing gets done two years later.
They're back to you.
And he said, after 30 years of it, we don't do it anymore in that way.
So he said that in order and he was right and he was right.
So it's those kind of things that make you feel like not only are you naive and I'm talking
about myself.
We can have all these great feelings about trying to change things but it takes careful
planning and a monstrous will.
I mean you never take no.
Someone says no to you.
You don't get upset.
You just you march on and you just do it again.
You have to be relentless and that's how we succeed it.
We didn't have it wasn't until 90 spring of 95 when Leon had made in and in that all the
sudden the sunpapers and everybody else was friggin' paying attention.
Where were they when we needed them so desperately back in 91, 92, 93, 94.
It was only until it was a fate of complete that they come in step in and say, oh yeah
we're lending the help and hand here.
And most amazing it wasn't until 96, 97 that we got the sports people of the sunpaper
to give any help at all and it happened to come through two very knowledgeable, highly
healed women who said we can't let this happen to Geraldine Day.
We can't let her sit in a cold apartment with no food.
Can you name these women?
Sure.
Let's put that on pause.
Okay.
The people that helped enormously at the sunpapers were the sports editor Molly Dunham,
DUNHAM and Sally McKee at the sunpapers and what they did came in about 1997 when Geraldine
Day was in deplorable situation with their help all together and I think we can say it
now because most of it has been, most of it she's had to spend to keep her act together
over $25,000 we raised on February 15th, 1997 for that particular event.
But they publicized it and they pushed it.
They actually even put in the newspapers that people wanted to donate something to donate
it directly to them.
The sunpapers to write it out to them and they'd see that Geraldine got the checks and I
think over $4,000 was raised that added to the other 20-some thousand made a big difference.
Really made a big difference.
That is the kindest thing that the sunpapers has done and again it didn't come from the
eyes that I thought would have had the sensitivity to it had to come through women.
Strange, huh?
Yeah, different.
But if they hadn't, if they hadn't, Geraldine would be in a much worse situation today
because we're in a situation again in which she's down to her last, this may sound like
a lot of money to you but it's not down to her last $6,000.
All that money we raised for.
And she's not going out there by and stuff.
If her medical bills and her, I mean, $20,000 or $1,000 over this will be, she spread it
over two years.
It's damn good for people's expenses, especially hers.
So we're going to have to do something again.
And I don't think we can do it the same way we did before because while we were doing
this fundraising, we were opposed in doing this fundraising by Joe Black.
Joe Black was a picture for the Brooklyn Dodgers, damn good one.
And he basically told us we were embarrassing, majorly baseball by coming out and saying,
you know, going over these things, so she's impoverished as medical, so fortunately, he
and I have mended our fences just this year.
I had to promise in order to do this, the men defense with Joe Black, that I would not,
if I'm doing a fundraiser, never do it for naming a specific player or a person like
Geraldine.
He says because they have an organization called Bat Baseball Assistance Team, which is helping
her.
And they're saying her a check for like $500 a month and that makes a very big difference
in her life.
And if that would stop, she'd be in real big trouble.
You're still currently involved with an organization for the New Year League.
Yes, called the Negro League Baseball Players Association.
We meet in Baltimore every, usually every month.
What is your position?
I'm just called an honorary board member.
Can you name some of the other people?
Oh, well, the players that are involved are the hundred and some players that are Negro
League players, that is played from 1920 to 1950.
And of course, that is ones that always attend the meetings are the President and Vice
President, Wilmer Fields and Stanley Glen, Bill Cash.
There are some others that aren't full-fledged Negro League players that attend those meetings.
At the last meeting, let's see, I'm trying to think of, there's a person that helps in
a sense of sponsoring these meetings so that we can have them as another white individual's
attorneys.
His name is Charles Winner.
His organization is Pro Bono, then helping for the last since 1995.
Somewhere around there and holding this organization together.
We meet in his offices once a month and a couple of months ago, we had any Murray, any
Murray is behind us with his brother, Charlie.
And we are slowly but surely getting some of the other Oriole players involved in this.
But then at the same time, there's another thing that may or may not happen in that who
is an individual came to us who is going to be inheriting a good deal of money.
And their initial idea was to do a film to set the record straight about the Negro League
players.
And also to have real pensions for these players.
As soon as she made these announcements, people within her family who did not have her feelings
along these lines hit the roof.
We don't know how long it's going to take for them to work out their economic situation.
This person is inheriting tens of millions of which she would like to funnel in this
direction.
You see, every time we make a step in a certain direction, it looks good and it holds
up for a while and then people from the outside who have never heard of this come in and say,
what do you want to do that?
Why do you want to help them?
I mean, you know, what they would do for you.
That's that kind of thing.
And then after a while, these people begin to feel very uneasy because other members and
their family and other people are saying, you know, come on, you're not black, why help
them and help you.
So come on, let's get on with their life.
Do other things.
So whenever that is resolved, if it is resolved in a positive way, we will be able to finally
get a decent pension for the whole of the guys, 170 of them.
Not just the rinky dink, few thousand dollars they're going to get per year for major
big baseball.
But a minimum of 10,000 a year, which isn't a great deal of money, but it's a whole lot
more than what they got.
So that's something we have in the background that we're working on.
And it comes in spurts.
I remember this person just the other day, it's just today.
And they're still going through hell.
There's politics and all of this.
When you get into big money, there's all these politics that are involved with people
coming through.
If here's a better deal for you, why don't you invest in this?
Why don't you invest?
That's understandable.
But hopefully we'll be able to iron this one out one last time.
But it's going to take a good year to it.
What would you say are the chief contributing factors to the rediscovery of the Negro
leagues?
Not necessarily your own personal discovery, but the undoubtedly.
It was an organization called Saber, the Society for the American Baseball Research.
They are really key to this whole thing.
It was their research that I read back then, 1990-91, that I began to realize that my
father was telling me that these were indeed some of the greatest baseball players ever.
So I think, what else did you ask?
I was asking about not necessarily your personal experience, but you say, well, we discovery
as a whole, people who are really conscious of this.
Again, there's a committee, the Negro League Committee of the Saber Society for American
Baseball Research is the most qualified group of individuals from the standpoint that they
followed this thing for 40, they're my teachers.
They're the ones that, and these are mostly all whites, again.
And they're the ones that, when I said, hey, I just went and talked to Tom Morris.
Tom Morris said to me, these guys weren't so good.
They played in band boxes in which 300 feet for the center field.
And then these other historians who were true historians, they said, well, where do you
get that information?
I mean, of course, he got it from some other friend who has his prejudice against blacks.
And that's a definite thing.
It happens.
Pregnance against blacks in this.
So from that perspective, I would have to say that that's the organization that did
most, has done the most in the spearheaded, this big change, and they get very little
credit.
I had very little credit.
Describe the initial reaction that Leende had prior to getting into the epine.
Well, as you'll read in this newsletter thing, I explained, I discussed the whole situation
in the deal that he and I had made.
And the deal that had I made was a Bob, you know, after I told him that Buck O'Neill
this was in the summer of 94.
And I told him.
I said, I couldn't tell him the name in person who gave me the information.
But you're in.
You're going to be voted in this year.
He didn't believe me.
This is 1994.
He had been through enough.
He was going through enough pain with prostate problems and everything else.
So he didn't believe me.
The funny thing he did, and this is very much not like Leende, and he said, okay, Bob,
I want you to be the person when it happens.
When you find out you come to me first, I want to talk to you first.
You tell me that I'm in and then I'll believe it.
So what happened is at the hospital, they were wheeling them out of some exploratory thing
that they were doing with them.
He was really a nun to good shape.
He had already had this incredible dream that he had already was in the Hall of Fame.
At seven o'clock in the morning, he called Geraldine and said, I'm made it, honey, I'm
made it.
And she reminded them that seven o'clock they haven't even met yet, and of course, he was
crestfallen.
But he was told, his soul told him, you know, and he believed it.
So when he was being wheeled out, we had previously made a deal on the inside with myself and
the other players, because I've always felt uneasy in this situation.
I'm not a player, right?
I'm their friend, but I'm not a player.
So we figured out, okay, who do we want to give this honor to?
We gave it to Max Mann, who was his teammate, his pitching teammate, which made sense.
And it looked good.
And it looked good for the media to have some black person telling, but I couldn't even
tell these guys what I promised to be on.
Because you want these fellows to get the most out of the situation you can.
It doesn't do much good if I say, look, I hate to tell you this, but Leon told me I had
to tell him from the cameras.
But that doesn't help them.
It doesn't help the whole image of the thing.
So when they wheeled him out, I was standing by, he said, and is the only time he's ever
called me Dr. Heranomus.
He yelled, Dr. Heranomus, where's Dr. Heranomus?
I want to speak to him.
So I went over to him and he looked at me and I knew what he was thinking and I said,
you're in.
And he said, and we had this on videotape.
This was really neat.
We didn't know we had it on videotape.
And he said, no kidding.
I said, yep, you're in.
So the last thing I saw of him is they were real.
He was trying to get up and he was looking at me like, okay, all right, I believe you.
I believe you.
But he didn't.
But he got into the room.
He was such a kind guy.
And Max Manning bent over and held his hand and said, you made it.
You made it.
That's when he believed it.
But he still said, no kid.
He still couldn't handle it.
It was truly, and I really was.
It was the most joyous moment in this fellow's life.
And for the last for six days was the pathetic situation of it.
Because I knew it was nip and tuck.
I couldn't tell the other guys.
I thought he was going to die before March 6 came.
I did.
I was very worried.
And after that, after putting all these other things together for him to celebrate it,
I always thought in my heart, he ain't never going to make it.
He ain't never going to see these things.
It was quite wonderful that he had those six days because he wasn't really going to
make it.
So that's really what happened.
They explained in this newsletter.
And I thought I had to set it straight.
I've listed all the names of the people that helped.
This was up to 1996 somewhere around there.
So you won't find their two names.
Because they didn't enter the picture until later.
They entered it after they realized that year two years, almost after his death, but
they realized that this can't go on.
So I think about these two women often.
Never met him.
But they were really on some heroes.
You cover it all?
I'm going to ask you more questions.
Well, I asked me one or two more.
We were colder or three.
What sticks out in your mind most about the accounts of the National Negro League players
had.
Like you said, Adair, when you were in the bus ride with them, I guess I'm asking you
basically for another anecdote.
Something.
Something.
OK, well, really impressed me was their memories.
All these bus trips that they take.
And we took a number of bus trips with them.
Their memories would be precise.
They would remember.
They would remember it looks on each other's faces when they were throwing the ball.
Which one was a strike?
And they would argue over, no, the first two were strikes, not two balls.
I mean, things like that.
And then you drop the ball.
You drop the ball and you lost that game for us.
I mean, it was more or less.
They would go through the games.
They would relive the games play by play.
Now I don't know if people do that anymore.
But that's truly remarkable.
After 30, 40 years to say he came out on the field and have a shoe on right, he bent down
to pick it up.
And after that, we knew we had him because we could tell by the way he was bending down.
He wasn't feeling so hot.
I mean, because he would observe everything about, you talk about guys that were fans
that watch everything.
These guys, they, it was a very strange situation because they not only watched their competition,
but they watched the other guys on the team because they were in competition with them.
To get out there on the field.
Because if they weren't out on the field and somebody else was, they weren't getting
paid.
So you know, they had no choice but to compete all the time with not just the opposition,
but themselves.
So it's their memories of all of these exacting things that I was overwhelmed with.
The fact that they remembered when they spit, they remembered, they remembered all the
little things that most people would think were inconsequential.
Because they loved what they were doing.
They really loved it.
I mean, this is the thing that is so important that most people are not aware of.
When you love what you do, you usually do your very best.
This may be one of them.
Those kind of things have always impressed me because they never, when they told these stories
about the games, you could see the games going on in your head.
They told them such a motion.
That's why we wanted to get them on film.
That's why we wanted to get them documented because it's the enthusiasm with which they
told them.
And they're very upset with the way Americans think Negro League Baseball was.
They don't like it at all.
They feel that they've been jipped.
They weren't clowns.
They weren't happy, they weren't lucky.
The struggles that they had to come up against.
I mean, some of the teams that they play about, they talk about dead balls.
You know what dead balls are?
Dead balls are when you freeze a baseball.
And when they would buy their baseballs, they had to buy their baseballs sometimes from
the team in which they were playing against.
So what these guys would do is they would take a gross of baseballs and put them in freezers
and give them to them just before the game.
At that ball, you can't hit.
You hit it and it dribbles.
It's the most you're going to get out of 100 feet or something like that.
So they had their own ways of determining whether they had a dead ball or not because of the
kind of people.
But you know, I don't know of any major league player that's ever given this any thought.
And so the quality of the shenanigans that were done to them, some of them especially
dealing with, you know, was legal to throw spit balls.
It was legal to throw cut balls, sandpaper balls, slime balls, needle balls, you know,
they stick a needle in there.
You know, this was football but baseball because when they played it, you know, you didn't
block any base path with these guys because if you were blocking the base, then the natural
thing to do is to jump up in the air and spike it, you know, to come down on you like
that.
And then you didn't block bases anymore.
You get block bases in white baseball, but not in black baseball.
The games that they play on the first basement, which is the same thing.
They had to be very careful about standing on the bag because the game that the runner
always had was the guy is standing.
If the guy is standing in the wrong place on the, on the bag, you got him.
You land on their foot, you know, you've been written here.
They're famous in their favorite place.
And that would put that guy out of commission and you'd get on that first base and go
on the second.
I mean, that's a desperate, pretty desperate, that's how they played.
I mean, I never would want to play against them.
Only because of that, because if they had it out for you, you were a dead man.
You know, they had some, they had, they had some violence and prejudices against each
other.
You know, when they called you a nigger, it meant something.
I'm not using the term in a derogatory sense.
I'm using the term that they use.
And they refer to players, certain players.
And I've heard Leon say this and I've heard some of the others.
They call him he's a nigger.
Which means, you know, not only as an asshole, but you could never trust him and he's not
even human.
He hasn't had, you know, the kind of, of the, well, it doesn't have, it's not the kind
of person that you would respect or want to be around with or anything like that.
They have a definite, you know, and it's really very strange when I was with a group of them
and they, when they come off with this.
Because I was always thought, never to use that term.
I thought it was a terrible derogatory term and I sure as hell never thought the blacks
would use that term.
But the disc group did, I don't know if they do anymore, disc group did.
And they did it for a very intentional purpose.
I mean, I'm hearing Monty Irvin and, and, and others, you know, Hall of Famers saying
oh, he's just a nigger.
Meaning that the, that, that, I'll give you an example.
There are a lot of black entrepreneurs that are not big ones, black entrepreneurs, but
small ones.
They have tried to make a buck off these players.
And we've had some here in Baltimore that have caused terrible situations.
And that's who they call niggers.
I remember the very first time I heard them say that.
I, I, I jumped back and they looked at me and they said, look, this is a person who we
don't want to have anything to do with.
And that's the term we use and we're always use.
So that kind of thing.
